reasoning: A volunteer youth leader demanding access to the private communications of minors without parental consent or organizational policy backing is a clear example of exceeding delegated authority. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an invasion of privacy and an abuse of the volunteer's limited supervisory role.

The authority is unambiguously illegitimate: a treasurer seizing control, suspending elections contrary to the constitution, and redirecting funds to a personal account is a clear usurpation of power. The fact that the denomination has explicitly voided his leadership removes any ambiguity a reasonable person might have about his right to enforce rules.

A religious group has no jurisdiction to enforce its internal modesty rules on a non-member's private property. The attempt to police a neighbor's backyard via surveillance and harassment is a clear overreach of authority that no reasonable person would defend.

The administrator is explicitly acting against written institutional policy (the handbook) to enforce a personal religious vendetta. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a Dean does not have the legitimate authority to hold standard transfer paperwork hostage for a personal 'confession session' when the student has met all official requirements.

No reasonable person would accept that a parent has the legitimate authority to withhold life-saving insulin from a Type 1 diabetic child based on a self-proclaimed status as a prophet. The authority's claim to power is baseless and the exercise of that power constitutes clear abuse and neglect rather than legitimate parenting or religious practice.

A private religious group has absolutely no legal authority to physically block a public county road or easement. No reasonable person would defend a private entity usurping control of public infrastructure to the detriment of residents' access rights.

The injustice is obvious because the delegate (dorm leader) is enforcing a rule that explicitly contradicts the higher authority's (college handbook) written policy. A reasonable person would immediately agree that a subordinate has no legitimate authority to conduct searches that their superiors have expressly forbidden them from performing without supervision.

The interim pastor is explicitly violating the organization's governing bylaws to retain power beyond his term and is actively suppressing the voting rights of the members. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as a usurpation of authority, rendering his restrictions on communication illegitimate.

An employer demanding access to personal bank statements to police legal, off-duty spending constitutes a clear overreach of authority, particularly when such surveillance is not stipulated in the employment contract. A reasonable person would immediately agree that an employer lacks the jurisdiction to audit private financial records for personal morality compliance.

The step-father clearly lacks the legal or moral authority to confiscate the personal property of an adult tenant who pays rent and purchased the items themselves. Asserting 'spiritual headship' to seize an adult's car and laptop is an obvious overreach that no reasonable person would defend as a legitimate exercise of household authority.
